title: Descriptive Statistics — Rincón
text: Notes on the descriptive statistics chapters from Luis Rincón’s probability and statistics textbook.

title: Descriptive Statistics — Rincón
text:
Test ggplot2

library(ggplot2)
source("theme_radicle_night.R")

# This is a comment
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Engine Displacement vs Highway MPG",
    x = "Displacement (L)",
    y = "Highway MPG"
  ) +
  scale_color_radicle_night() +
  theme_radicle_night()

title: Building a Net Worth Tracker with R + Shiny
text: Organizing Personal Finances — invest-safely.com

title: Building a Net Worth Tracker with R + Shiny
text: Organizing Personal Finances — invest-safely.com

title: Building a Net Worth Tracker with R + Shiny
text:
The framework
The article organizes personal finances around Robert Kiyosaki’s cashflow model — two statements, not one:

Income Statement: earned income vs. expenses.
Balance Sheet: assets vs. liabilities.

It splits assets into two flavors that matter a lot more than their dollar value:

Personal-use assets — house you live in, car you drive. They don’t generate cashflow.
Income-generating assets — rentals, dividend stocks, businesses, royalties. These pay you.

And it frames four cashflow patterns as stages, not labels:







Stage
Pattern




Poor
Earned income, spent almost immediately on consumption


Middle Class
Earned income, offset by liabilities (mortgage, car loans, credit cards)


Rich
Earned income + growing passive/portfolio income from income-generating assets


Wealthy
Passive/portfolio income alone covers all expenses — earned income becomes optional



The mechanism for moving up a stage isn’t “earn more,” it’s directing leftover cashflow into income-generating assets instead of liabilities or consumption. That’s the number worth tracking over time, which is why net worth alone is an incomplete picture — the composition of assets matters as much as the total.
The two statements connect through cashflow — this is the diagram the article is describing in words:





%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {
  'darkMode': true, 'background': '#1a1b26',
  'primaryColor': '#24252d', 'primaryTextColor': '#f9f9fb', 'primaryBorderColor': '#9b9bb1',
  'lineColor': '#9b9bb1', 'edgeLabelBackground': '#16161e'
}}}%%
flowchart TB
    EI[Earned Income] --> CF{{Monthly Cashflow}}
    PI[Passive / Portfolio Income] --> CF
    CF --> EX[Expenses]
    CF -->|leftover, good direction| IGA[Income-generating Assets]
    CF -->|leftover, bad direction| LIA[Liabilities]
    IGA -.pays back.-> PI
    LIA -.demands more.-> EX
    PUA[Personal-use Assets] -.no cashflow.-> CF

    style EI fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style PI fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style CF fill:#16161e,stroke:#bbeeff,color:#bbeeff
    style EX fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style IGA fill:#99f9d9,stroke:#1a1b26,color:#1a1b26
    style LIA fill:#f999f9,stroke:#1a1b26,color:#1a1b26
    style PUA fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb






Leftover cashflow routed into income-generating assets compounds back into passive income (closing the loop on the left); routed into liabilities it compounds into more expenses instead (closing the loop on the right). Same dollar, opposite direction. The four stages are just how far along that left-hand loop someone is:





%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {
  'darkMode': true, 'background': '#1a1b26',
  'primaryColor': '#24252d', 'primaryTextColor': '#f9f9fb', 'primaryBorderColor': '#9b9bb1',
  'lineColor': '#9b9bb1', 'edgeLabelBackground': '#16161e'
}}}%%
flowchart LR
    Poor -->|"cut consumption"| MC[Middle Class]
    MC -->|"redirect leftover cashflow\nfrom liabilities to assets"| Rich
    Rich -->|"passive income > expenses"| Wealthy

    style Poor fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style MC fill:#9999ff,stroke:#1a1b26,color:#1a1b26
    style Rich fill:#bbeeff,stroke:#1a1b26,color:#1a1b26
    style Wealthy fill:#99f9d9,stroke:#1a1b26,color:#1a1b26






The source article doesn’t prescribe a spreadsheet or tool — it points at the board game Cashflow 101 as a way to internalize the pattern. Below is a step-by-step for turning the same framework into an actual R/Shiny tracker.

title: Building a Net Worth Tracker with R + Shiny
section: What the tracker needs to capture
text:
What the tracker needs to capture
Net worth alone hides the story. The app should track, per entry:

Date — net worth is a time series, not a snapshot.
Type — Asset or Liability.
Asset subtype — Income-generating or Personal-use (only applies to assets). This is what lets the app show progress toward the “Rich” stage, not just a bigger number.
Category — e.g. Cash, Investments, Real Estate (rental vs. primary residence), Vehicle, Credit Card, Mortgage, Student Loan.
Description and Value.

Net worth = sum(assets) − sum(liabilities). The more interesting derived metric is % of net worth in income-generating assets, since that’s the Kiyosaki-stage indicator.

title: Building a Net Worth Tracker with R + Shiny
text:
App architecture
How an entry travels from the sidebar form to the three output tabs:





%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {
  'darkMode': true, 'background': '#1a1b26',
  'primaryColor': '#24252d', 'primaryTextColor': '#f9f9fb', 'primaryBorderColor': '#9b9bb1',
  'lineColor': '#9b9bb1', 'edgeLabelBackground': '#16161e'
}}}%%
flowchart LR
    UI["Sidebar form\n(date, type, subtype, category, value)"] -->|dbExecute INSERT| DB[(SQLite\nentries table)]
    DB -->|dbReadTable| RE["reactiveVal: entries()"]
    RE --> NW["net_worth_by_date()"]
    RE --> PCT["pct_income_generating()"]
    RE --> LED["Ledger tab (DT)"]
    NW --> TR["Overview tab:\nnet worth trend"]
    PCT --> CO["Composition tab:\nasset pie chart"]

    style UI fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style DB fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style RE fill:#9999ff,stroke:#1a1b26,color:#1a1b26
    style NW fill:#16161e,stroke:#bbeeff,color:#bbeeff
    style PCT fill:#16161e,stroke:#bbeeff,color:#bbeeff
    style LED fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style TR fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb
    style CO fill:#24252d,stroke:#9b9bb1,color:#f9f9fb






Everything downstream of entries() is a pure transformation of the same table — the trend chart, the composition pie, and the ledger never talk to SQLite directly, only to that one reactive. That’s what step 4 below sets up.

title: Building a Net Worth Tracker with R + Shiny
text:
Step by step

1. Project setup
# from the notes/ project root, or wherever you want the tracker to live
usethis::create_project("net_worth_tracker")
install.packages(c(
  "shiny", "bslib", "DT", "tidyverse",
  "DBI", "RSQLite", "plotly", "shinyWidgets"
))
Use renv::init() if you want the environment pinned — worth it for anything you’ll keep adding data to over months.


2. Data model and persistence
SQLite over CSV: it survives concurrent writes better and makes querying by date range trivial. One table is enough.
library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "net_worth.sqlite")

dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS entries (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    entry_date TEXT NOT NULL,
    type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (type IN ('Asset', 'Liability')),
    subtype TEXT CHECK (subtype IN ('Income-generating', 'Personal-use') OR subtype IS NULL),
    category TEXT NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    value REAL NOT NULL
  )
")
subtype is nullable because it only applies to assets — liabilities don’t get it.


3. App skeleton
# app.R
library(shiny)
library(bslib)
library(DT)
library(dplyr)
library(plotly)
library(DBI)
library(RSQLite)

con <- dbConnect(SQLite(), "net_worth.sqlite")

ui <- page_sidebar(
  title = "Net Worth Tracker",
  theme = bs_theme(bootswatch = "darkly"),
  sidebar = sidebar(
    dateInput("entry_date", "Date", value = Sys.Date()),
    radioButtons("type", "Type", choices = c("Asset", "Liability")),
    conditionalPanel(
      "input.type == 'Asset'",
      radioButtons("subtype", "Subtype",
                   choices = c("Income-generating", "Personal-use"))
    ),
    textInput("category", "Category"),
    textInput("description", "Description"),
    numericInput("value", "Value", value = 0, min = 0),
    actionButton("add", "Add entry", class = "btn-primary")
  ),
  navset_tab(
    nav_panel("Overview", uiOutput("summary_cards"), plotlyOutput("networth_trend")),
    nav_panel("Composition", plotlyOutput("asset_composition")),
    nav_panel("Ledger", DTOutput("ledger"))
  )
)


4. Server logic — writing entries
server <- function(input, output, session) {

  observeEvent(input$add, {
    subtype <- if (input$type == "Asset") input$subtype else NA

    dbExecute(con, "
      INSERT INTO entries (entry_date, type, subtype, category, description, value)
      VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)",
      params = list(
        as.character(input$entry_date), input$type, subtype,
        input$category, input$description, input$value
      )
    )

    entries(dbReadTable(con, "entries"))  # refresh reactive below
  })

  entries <- reactiveVal(dbReadTable(con, "entries"))


5. Server logic — the numbers that matter
  net_worth_by_date <- reactive({
    entries() |>
      mutate(
        signed_value = if_else(type == "Asset", value, -value),
        entry_date = as.Date(entry_date)
      ) |>
      arrange(entry_date) |>
      group_by(entry_date) |>
      summarise(delta = sum(signed_value), .groups = "drop") |>
      mutate(net_worth = cumsum(delta))
  })

  pct_income_generating <- reactive({
    df <- entries() |> filter(type == "Asset")
    if (nrow(df) == 0) return(0)
    ig <- sum(df$value[df$subtype == "Income-generating"])
    ig / sum(df$value)
  })
pct_income_generating is the Kiyosaki-stage signal from the article — rising over time means cashflow is going into assets that pay you, not just accumulating stuff or debt.


6. Visualizations
  output$networth_trend <- renderPlotly({
    p <- ggplot(net_worth_by_date(), aes(entry_date, net_worth)) +
      geom_line(color = "#99f9d9", linewidth = 1) +
      geom_point(color = "#99f9d9") +
      labs(x = NULL, y = "Net Worth", title = "Net worth over time")
    ggplotly(p)
  })

  output$asset_composition <- renderPlotly({
    df <- entries() |>
      filter(type == "Asset") |>
      group_by(subtype) |>
      summarise(total = sum(value), .groups = "drop")

    plot_ly(df, labels = ~subtype, values = ~total, type = "pie",
            marker = list(colors = c("#99f9d9", "#9999ff")))
  })
Reuse theme_radicle_night.R and its palette here (scale_color_radicle_night(), scale_fill_radicle_night()) if you’re plotting with base ggplot2 instead of plotly directly — keeps it visually consistent with the rest of this site.


7. Ledger view
  output$ledger <- renderDT({
    datatable(entries(), options = list(order = list(list(1, "desc"))))
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)


8. Running it
shiny::runApp("net_worth_tracker")
For anything beyond local use, rsconnect::deployApp() to shinyapps.io keeps it reachable from a phone for monthly updates — a tracker only works if updating it takes under a minute.


9. Extending later

Snapshots vs. transactions: the model above stores every entry as a delta. An alternative is snapshotting total asset/liability values monthly — simpler net worth math, but you lose the “what changed” detail. Pick one; don’t mix them.
Multiple currencies: add a currency column and convert at read time with a fixed or fetched exchange rate — don’t store converted values.
Goals: a horizontal geom_hline on the trend chart for a target net worth, or a target pct_income_generating, turns the app from a ledger into a scoreboard.







Note



This is a build plan, not a finished app — swap in your own categories and run the CREATE TABLE once to get started.

title: Hands-On Programming with R
text:
Grolemund, G. (2014). Hands-On Programming with R: Write Your Own Functions and Simulations. O’Reilly Media.

Book site: https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/

tidyquant: Tidy Quantitative Financial Analysis by Matt Dancho

Site: https://business-science.github.io/tidyquant/index.html

title: Hands-On Programming with R
text:
Grolemund, G. (2014). Hands-On Programming with R: Write Your Own Functions and Simulations. O’Reilly Media.

Book site: https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/

tidyquant: Tidy Quantitative Financial Analysis by Matt Dancho

Site: https://business-science.github.io/tidyquant/index.html

title: Hands-On Programming with R
text:
Purpose
Practice the concepts in Hands-On Programming with R using datasets obtained with the tidyquant library.

title: Hands-On Programming with R
text:
Quick Overview of TidyQuant
tidyquant is an R package that integrates quantitative financial analysis with the tidyverse, it pulls financial/economic data via quantmod, TTR and xst and let us work with it using tidy, pipeable workflows for charting, technical analysis and portfolio/performance analytics.

1. Install

install.packages("tidyquant")



2. Load it

library(tidyquant)

This also loads core tidyverse pieces (dplyr, ggplot2, tidyr, purrr, lubridate) plus xts, quantmod, TTR, PerformanceAnalytics.


3. Pull some data

# See what indexes are available
tq_index_options()

[1] "DOW"       "DOWGLOBAL" "SP400"     "SP500"     "SP600"    

# Pull the full S&P 500 constituent list
sp500 <- tq_index("SP500")

Getting holdings for SP500

head(sp500)

# A tibble: 6 × 8
  symbol company       identifier sedol weight sector shares_held local_currency
  <chr>  <chr>         <chr>      <chr>  <dbl> <chr>        <dbl> <chr>         
1 NVDA   NVIDIA CORP   67066G104  2379… 0.0789 -        295372891 USD           
2 AAPL   APPLE INC     037833100  2046… 0.0737 -        179114962 USD           
3 MSFT   MICROSOFT CO… 594918104  2588… 0.0450 -         90592557 USD           
4 AMZN   AMAZON.COM I… 023135106  2000… 0.0382 -        119381282 USD           
5 GOOGL  ALPHABET INC… 02079K305  BYVY… 0.0333 -         71510987 USD           
6 AVGO   BROADCOM INC  11135F101  BDZ7… 0.0286 -         57742540 USD           


This is all what we will need for this practice.

title: Hands-On Programming with R
text:
Practice

Chapter 2 - The Very Basics
I’m going to skip the very very basics, like choose an editor and set it up…
1. Objects & assignment (<-)
To create an R object, choose a name and then use the less-than symbol, <, followed by a minus sign, -, to save data into it. Using the previous code,

sp500 <- tq_index("SP500")
# sp500 is the object that we create to store the pulled data
names(sp500)

2. Vectors & element-wise operations
Well, what is a vector? In mathematics, a vector is an object that has both magnitude (size/length) and direction. It’s typically represented as an arrow, or numerically as an ordered list (tuple) of numbers called components or coordinates.
In R this has more to do with an ordered list, using the same tibble that we use before sp500$symbol is just a numeric vector for R.

# We can show all the elements just running
# sp500$weight
# But to save space we will use head() function
head(sp500$weight, 10) 

 [1] 0.07886066 0.07370111 0.04503111 0.03824187 0.03332606 0.02860431
 [7] 0.02681145 0.02292649 0.01701708 0.01562640


this shows us the first 10 elements in the vector sp500$weight.
Now we will run an aritmetic operatin with this vector. When R performs element-wise execution, it matches up vectors and then manipulates each pair of elements independently. We will do sp500$weight + 1 and this works
0.07886066 + 1 = 1.07886066
0.07370111 + 1 = 1.07370111
0.04503111 + 1 = 1.04503111
0.03824187 + 1 = 1.03824187
…

# Here R will performs element-wise execution
# This adds 1 to each element in the vector sp500$weight
head(sp500$weight + 1, 10) 

 [1] 1.078861 1.073701 1.045031 1.038242 1.033326 1.028604 1.026811 1.022926
 [9] 1.017017 1.015626


2. Functions
